notes.title: Data Structures Overview
notes.content:
Arrays

Fixed-size sequential memory. O(1) random access by index. O(n) insert/delete at arbitrary positions. Best for random access patterns.

Linked Lists

Dynamic size. O(1) insert/delete at head. O(n) random access. Singly vs doubly linked. Useful when size changes frequently.

Hash Tables

Key-value store. O(1) average for insert, delete, lookup. Collision handling via chaining or open addressing. Load factor matters for performance.

Trees

BST: O(log n) search/insert/delete on average. Balanced variants (AVL, Red-Black) guarantee O(log n) worst case.

Heaps: Priority queues. O(1) find-min, O(log n) extract-min. Implemented as array.

Graphs

BFS: Shortest path (unweighted). Uses a queue. O(V+E). Level-order traversal.

DFS: Cycle detection, topological sort. Uses stack or recursion. O(V+E).

Dijkstra: Shortest path with non-negative weights. O((V+E) log V) with min-heap.

notes.title: React Hooks Cheatsheet
notes.content:
useState

Manage local state. Returns [value, setter]. Re-renders component when state changes.

useEffect

Side effects: data fetching, subscriptions, DOM mutation. Runs after render. Return cleanup function for subscriptions.

useEffect(() => {
  const sub = subscribe(id);
  return () => sub.unsubscribe();
}, [id]);

useCallback

Memoize a function. Prevents re-creating the same function on every render. Useful when passing callbacks to memoized children.

useMemo

Memoize the result of an expensive computation. Only recomputes when dependencies change. Don't overuse — the memoization itself has a cost.

useRef

Persist a mutable value without triggering a re-render. Also used to access DOM elements directly.

useContext

Consume context value without prop drilling. Pair with createContext and a Provider component.

Custom Hooks

Extract stateful logic into reusable functions. Must start with use. Can call other hooks.

notes.title: TypeScript Tips
notes.content:
Type vs Interface

Interface: Extendable via extends, declaration merging. Best for object shapes and class contracts.

Type: More flexible — unions, intersections, mapped types, conditional types. Can't be merged.

Utility Types

  • Partial<T> — all properties optional
  • Required<T> — all properties required
  • Pick<T, K> — subset of properties
  • Omit<T, K> — exclude specific properties
  • Record<K, V> — key-value map type
  • Readonly<T> — immutable properties
  • ReturnType<T> — infer return type of function

Generics

Write reusable, type-safe code. Use extends for constraints. Prefer explicit type parameters over any.

Type Guards

Narrow types at runtime: typeof, instanceof, in operator, or user-defined guards returning value is Type.

Discriminated Unions

Add a kind or type literal field to each variant. TypeScript narrows automatically in switch statements.

notes.title: Big O Notation
notes.content:
Time Complexity (best to worst)

  • O(1) — Constant: array index access, hash table lookup
  • O(log n) — Logarithmic: binary search, balanced BST operations
  • O(n) — Linear: linear scan, single loop
  • O(n log n) — Linearithmic: merge sort, heap sort, efficient comparison sorting
  • O(n²) — Quadratic: bubble sort, insertion sort, naive string matching
  • O(2ⁿ) — Exponential: recursive Fibonacci, power set generation
  • O(n!) — Factorial: brute-force permutations, traveling salesman

Space Complexity

Recursion depth adds O(depth) stack frames. Iterative solutions usually save space. DFS on a tree: O(h) stack, BFS: O(w) queue.

Amortized Analysis

Dynamic array append: O(1) amortized even though occasional doubling is O(n). Total cost spread over all operations.

Best / Average / Worst Case

QuickSort: O(n log n) average, O(n²) worst. Randomized pivot selection makes worst case very unlikely in practice.

Drop Constants Rule

O(2n) = O(n). O(n² + n) = O(n²). Big O describes growth rate, not exact cost.

notes.title: REST API Best Practices
notes.content:
URL Design

Use nouns, not verbs. Plural for collections. Hierarchical to show relationships.

  • GET /users — list users
  • GET /users/:id — get one user
  • POST /users — create user
  • PUT /users/:id — full replace
  • PATCH /users/:id — partial update
  • DELETE /users/:id — delete

HTTP Status Codes

  • 200 OK, 201 Created, 204 No Content
  • 400 Bad Request, 401 Unauthorized, 403 Forbidden, 404 Not Found
  • 409 Conflict, 422 Unprocessable Entity, 429 Too Many Requests
  • 500 Internal Server Error, 503 Service Unavailable

Versioning

URL versioning (/api/v1/) is most explicit. Header versioning is cleaner but harder to test. Be explicit about deprecation timelines.

Pagination

Cursor-based for large / real-time datasets. Offset-based is simpler but inconsistent under mutations. Include next/prev links in response.

Error Format

Consistent error envelope: { error: { code, message, details } }. Never leak stack traces in production.

notes.title: System Design: Scalability
notes.content:
Horizontal vs Vertical Scaling

Vertical: Bigger machine. Simpler, no code change, but has a ceiling and a single point of failure.

Horizontal: More machines. Requires load balancing and stateless services. Scales linearly.

Load Balancing

Algorithms: round-robin, least connections, IP hash (session stickiness). Tools: Nginx, HAProxy, AWS ALB. Health checks matter.

Caching

CDN: Edge caching for static assets — dramatically cuts latency globally.

Redis: In-memory cache for computed results, session data, rate limiting. Cache invalidation is the hardest problem.

Cache-aside, read-through, write-through patterns — choose based on consistency requirements.

Database Scaling

Read replicas: Route reads to replicas. Eventual consistency. Works for 90% of read-heavy cases.

Sharding: Horizontal partition by a shard key. Consistent hashing minimises resharding pain.

Async Processing

Message queues (Kafka, RabbitMQ, SQS) decouple services, absorb traffic spikes, and enable retries. Prefer async for anything that doesn't need an immediate response.

CAP Theorem

Consistency, Availability, Partition Tolerance — pick 2. In practice: partition tolerance is non-negotiable, so choose between strong consistency (CP: PostgreSQL, MongoDB) and high availability (AP: Cassandra, DynamoDB).

notes.title: Hackathon: Lily Memo Demo Pitch
notes.content:
Product Overview

Lily Memo is an AI-powered study companion PWA. Local-first with IndexedDB — works fully offline. Optional cross-device sync via encrypted Redis.

Key Features to Demo

  • Rich text note-taking with AI analysis (Lily)
  • Study session timer — stopwatch and Pomodoro modes
  • Weekly calendar for scheduling tasks
  • Progress tracking with gamified badge system
  • English / Japanese bilingual interface
  • Backup & restore — full data portability, no lock-in

Tech Stack

  • Next.js 16 + React 19 + TypeScript
  • Dexie (IndexedDB) for local storage
  • Gemini AI (server proxy in English mode)
  • Upstash Redis for optional live sync
  • Deployed on Vercel

Demo Script

  1. Open app in English mode — show note creation
  2. Ask Lily to analyse a note → AI response
  3. Start a 25-min Pomodoro timer
  4. Open Calendar view → add a task for today
  5. Show Badge room — earned badges from study history
  6. Download backup → restore on second device

Why Lily Memo?

Students need a focused, intelligent study tool that respects privacy. No account required, no cloud dependency for core features — your data stays on your device.
